summary: Camille Fournier argues that while engineering managers have diverse strengths, the non-negotiable skill is creating highly productive teams. She identifies three core competencies: breaking down project scope to ship frequently, balancing delivery with sustaining engineering to manage technical debt, and ruthless prioritization. The post emphasizes that failing in these areas leads to disengaged teams, burnout, and purposelessness. While acknowledging other management skills matter, she maintains that delivery focus is fundamental to the role.
key_points: Engineering productivity is the one non-optional characteristic of great engineering managers, regardless of their other strengths
Project management should not be handed off to professional PMs for work within a single team - it's essential to engineering leadership development
Technical debt 'inevitably comes due unless you declare bankruptcy' - the investment in sustaining engineering depends on future certainty
Teams that don't ship become disengaged and miss the positive feedback of seeing work come to fruition
Nurturing healthy teams and helping them deliver their best work are interconnected, not competing priorities
money_quotes: A great engineering manager is capable of creating a highly productive engineering team.
If you are not great at getting your team to be productive, this is a critical growth opportunity.
Ask any engineering manager at a startup what one of their most dreaded questions is, and they will almost certainly mention 'why isn't it done yet?'

This is why I think project management is such an important part of engineering leadership development, and why I hate to hand it off to professional project managers for work that doesn't cross teams or organizations.
There's a reason we call it 'technical debt' and that is because it inevitably comes due, unless you declare bankruptcy.
Teams who don't ship are usually disengaged and rarely get the positive feedback of seeing their work come to fruition.
If your team isn't shipping, you're not doing your job.
Nurturing a safe and healthy team helps your team do their best work, and helping them identify and deliver that best work is a key part of keeping them healthy.
key_insight: Engineering managers must master productivity and delivery - without it, even the best people management skills leave teams disengaged and purposeless.
